name: Oroel Ascent Trail
name_original: Peña Oroel – trail running desde el Parador de Oroel
description: Tackle the difficult Peña Oroel trail running route from Parador de Oroel, featuring 5.3 miles and 2260 feet of elevation gain.
region_intro: Jogging around Jaca offers diverse landscapes, from challenging mountain trails in the Pyrenees to gentler paths through valleys and forests. The region is characterized by significant elevation changes, particularly around prominent features like Peña Oroel, providing varied terrain for runners. Paths often follow natural water features and pass through wooded areas, catering to different fitness levels. This blend of rugged mountains and serene valleys makes Jaca a prime destination for outdoor running. Best jogging routes around Jaca The most popular jogging route is Oroel Ascent Trail, a difficult 5.3 miles (8.5 km) trail that takes 1 hour 36 minutes to complete. This route offers a challenging ascent with substantial elevation gain towards Peña Oroel. Another top favourite among local runners is Fuerte de Rapitán - circular trail running from the Hospital de Jaca, a difficult 4.1 miles (6.6 km) path. This route integrates a historical fort with scenic views of Jaca. Local runners also love the Ipas - circular trail running from Jaca, a 6.3 miles (10.2 km) trail leading through the local countryside, often completed in about 1 hour 17 minutes. Jogging around Jaca is defined by Pyrenean mountain scenery, picturesque valleys, and wooded promenades. The network offers options for various ability levels, from moderate runs to difficult trail challenges. The routes in Jaca are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from 13 reviews. More than 2000 runners have used komoot to explore Jaca's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available around Jaca?
A: Jaca offers a wide selection of running routes, with over 100 trails available. These range from gentle paths through valleys to challenging mountain ascents in the Pyrenees, catering to all levels of runners.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the jogging trails near Jaca?
A: The jogging trails around Jaca feature diverse terrain. You'll find everything from rugged mountain paths with significant elevation changes, especially around prominent features like Peña Oroel, to gentler routes through picturesque valleys, forests, and along riverbanks. Many paths also incorporate historical sites and offer stunning Pyrenean views.

Q: Are there running routes suitable for beginners in Jaca?
A: While Jaca is known for its challenging mountain trails, there are also routes suitable for beginners. You can find moderate trails that offer a less strenuous experience, often through wooded areas or along promenades. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24665011" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fountain at the Cantera promenade – View of the Pyrenees loop from Jaca&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate option with beautiful views.

Q: What is the best time of year for trail running in Jaca?
A: Jaca's diverse climate means different seasons offer unique running experiences. Spring and autumn are generally ideal, with pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery. Summer mornings and evenings are also great, especially for higher-altitude trails. Winter can be challenging due to snow in the mountains, but lower-lying paths may still be accessible.

Q: Are there challenging mountain runs in the Jaca area?
A: Absolutely. Jaca is nestled in the Pyrenees, offering numerous challenging mountain runs. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e949739703" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Oroel Ascent Trail&lt;/a&gt; provide significant elevation gain and panoramic views. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14942538" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parador de Oroel – Peña Oroel Summit loop from Jaca&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you to the summit of Peña Oroel.

Q: Can I find circular running routes in Jaca?
A: Yes, many of the running routes around Jaca are circular, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e949597694" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuerte de Rapitán - circular trail running from the Hospital de Jaca&lt;/a&gt; offers a loop that includes a historical fort. Another popular circular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e949588092" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ipas - circular trail running from Jaca&lt;/a&gt;, which explores the local countryside.

Q: Are there running paths with historical landmarks or scenic viewpoints?
A: Many running paths in Jaca offer a blend of natural beauty and historical interest. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e949597694" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuerte de Rapitán - circular trail running from the Hospital de Jaca&lt;/a&gt; takes you past the historic Fort Rapitán with scenic views. You can also find trails that follow sections of the Camino de Santiago, offering both historical significance and picturesque scenery. For breathtaking vistas, consider routes leading to &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2839913" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Oroel Summit and Cross&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What natural features or attractions can I see while running in Jaca?
A: While running in Jaca, you can encounter stunning Pyrenean landscapes, including majestic mountains and serene valleys like the Aragon River valley. Some routes pass by beautiful lakes, such as those mentioned in the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/guide/1530595/lakes-around-jaca" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lakes around Jaca guide&lt;/a&gt;, or offer views of prominent peaks like those in the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/guide/1530590/peaks-around-jaca" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peaks around Jaca guide&lt;/a&gt;. You might also spot highlights like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/565433" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Refuge d'Ayous&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4473222" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Piedrafita Lake&lt;/a&gt; on longer excursions.

Q: Is parking available near the trailheads in Jaca?
A: Yes, parking is generally available near many of the popular trailheads and starting points for running routes in and around Jaca. Specific parking information can often be found on individual route descriptions on komoot, or by checking local signage in areas like the Hospital de Jaca or near the town center.

Q: Are the running trails around Jaca accessible by public transport?
A: Jaca itself is a well-connected town, and some running routes may be accessible directly from the town center or via local bus services that connect to nearby villages. For trails further afield in the Pyrenees, public transport options might be more limited, and a car would be recommended for easier access to trailheads.

Q: Are there family-friendly jogging options in Jaca?
A: Yes, Jaca offers several family-friendly jogging options. Paths along the Aragon River valley or through wooded promenades provide gentler, flatter terrain suitable for families. These routes often have less elevation gain and are perfect for a relaxed run or walk with children.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the running trails in Jaca?
A: Many trails in the Jaca region are dog-friendly, allowing you to enjoy a run with your canine companion. However, it's always advisable to keep your dog on a leash, especially in nature reserves, agricultural areas, or where wildlife might be present. Always check specific trail regulations or local signage for any restrictions.

Q: What do other runners say about the trails in Jaca?
A: The running trails in Jaca are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars from 13 reviews. Over 2000 runners have explored the area, often praising the stunning Pyrenean scenery, the variety of terrain, and the well-maintained paths that cater to different fitness levels.
tips: The ascent to the Cruz de Oroel from the parador consists of 33 curves through a leafy forest. The corresponding number is written on each of them so that hikers can keep track. It is a rocky trail where, if you are going to trail ride, you have to pay attention to the trunks and roots, especially on the way down.

It is difficult to find a person from Jaca who has never climbed the Oroel rock.
The silhouette of this mountain presides over the Aragón valley and stars in all the postcards of the city.
At its top, at 1769 meters high, there is a large iron cross of 10.4 meters. It was built in 1901 with the aim of being visible from Jaca and was paid for with donations from the residents themselves.
Old hermitage inside the rock where they say that the virgin was appreciated by a shepherd

In the parador de la Peña Oroel there is a fresh water fountain, an area with picnic areas under trees and a very complete panoramic view of Jaca with the Pyrenees in the background.
Currently the restaurant is closed, but some summers it opens and serves typical food from the Aragonese Pyrenees, mainly migas.
On good weather days, it is common to see many families having picnics in the area, as there is a children's playground next to the picnic areas.
It is also the most popular point from which to face the climb to Peña Oroel. From here the famous 33 curves to the cross begin.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete the Peña Oroel trail running route?
faqs.answer: The route is approximately 8.5 km long with significant elevation gain. For trail running, it typically takes around 1 hour and 36 minutes to complete, depending on your pace and fitness level.

faqs.question: Where is the starting point for this trail running route and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The trail starts directly from the Parador de Oroel. There is parking available at the Parador, which also offers a viewpoint, a fresh water fountain, and picnic areas, making it a convenient base for your activity.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for beginner trail runners?
faqs.answer: This trail is rated as difficult for trail running due to its sustained ascent and uneven, rocky terrain. While it's a rewarding experience, beginners might find it very challenging. Some sections are steep enough to require walking, even for experienced runners.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Peña Oroel trail?
faqs.answer: The terrain is varied and challenging. You'll encounter a continuous and steady climb through dense forests. The path is often rocky, with stony and stepped sections, including natural steps formed by roots, especially on the descent. The main ascent from the Parador follows 'The 33 Bends of Oroel' through a leafy forest.

faqs.question: What kind of views can I expect along the route?
faqs.answer: As you ascend, the forest provides a beautiful natural setting. From the ridge and especially at the summit, you'll be treated to spectacular 360° views of the surrounding Pyrenees mountains, the Aragón valley, and the city of Jaca below. The southern slopes offer expansive views where the forest covers everything.

faqs.question: What notable landmarks or features will I encounter on the trail?
faqs.answer: Key features include The 33 Bends of Oroel, which help mark your progress. At the peak, you'll find the prominent Peña Oroel Summit and Cross, a large 10.4-meter iron cross visible from Jaca. You'll also pass by the Hermitage of the Virgin of the Cave and the Parador de Oroel Viewpoint at the start.

faqs.question: What are the best times of year to trail run Peña Oroel?
faqs.answer: Spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions for trail running, with milder temperatures and beautiful foliage. Summer can be hot, so an early start is recommended. Winter conditions can bring snow and ice, making the trail significantly more challenging and requiring appropriate gear and experience.

faqs.question: Is the Peña Oroel trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, the trail is generally dog-friendly. However, as it is located within the Paisaje Protegido de San Juan de la Peña y Monte Oroel, it is important to keep your dog on a leash to protect local wildlife and ensure a pleasant experience for all trail users.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required to access the trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to access the Peña Oroel trail. It is freely accessible to the public. However, as it's part of the Paisaje Protegido de San Juan de la Peña y Monte Oroel, please respect all natural regulations, such as staying on marked paths and not disturbing wildlife.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates sections of several official trails. You will follow parts of the "Subida a Oroel" and "Camino a la Cruz de Oroel." Additionally, the trail passes near the "Ermita de la Cueva" and includes segments of the "Senda de los Lobos," which is a popular and scenic alternative route.

faqs.question: Is the Peña Oroel trail a loop or an out-and-back route?
faqs.answer: The described route is primarily an out-and-back to the summit. However, there are options to create a loop by incorporating other paths like the "Senda de los Lobos" for the return, which is described as a very beautiful, though potentially narrow and technical, alternative.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities or places to eat near the trailhead?
faqs.answer: The starting point at the Parador de Oroel offers basic amenities like a viewpoint, a fresh water fountain, and picnic areas. For more extensive facilities, including cafes, pubs, and accommodation, you would need to head to the nearby city of Jaca, which is a short drive away.

waypoints.name: Hermitage of the Virgin of the Cave
